Stumps and waterlogged trunks provide the basis for hundreds of varieties of mosses and mildew, behind which spring a curtain of high reeds. This tiny cul-de-sac is a dead end for all but the smallest of birds and creatures.

node.name: The Faldesu River, Shallows
node.description: Leading away from this small, reed-cluttered shore, a long sandy trail winds its way through clumps of vegetation toward somewhat drier land. The water here is merely ankle deep, though the mud beneath the surface makes movement difficult -- too shallow to actively swim, but too wet to actually walk. Arboreal creatures, such as the occasional squirrel, have a much easier time traversing this area through the overhanging branches.

node.name: The Faldesu River, Shallows
node.description: Curving out into the water is a small, muddy extension of the bank, so choked with reeds that only the smallest of birds could find passage through it. Eternally, the water laps away at this feature, sucking away the black mud to expose stubborn roots. Though eventually the river may win, the tiny outlet of land will not go quietly into that dark water.

node.name: The Faldesu River, Shallows
node.description: A sandbar humps out of the Faldesu's waters, making it impossible to approach the shore by boat. Driftwood jutting out of the mud is a tell-tale sign that at some point, at least one captain tried to beach a vessel here, though to what goal is impossible to guess. In the time it took for nature to reduce the wreck into fragments, it would appear that no others lacked the sanity enough to try.

node.name: The Faldesu River, Shallows
node.description: Something has made several nests in the muddy banks, each spaced out some distance from one another and only partially excavated. Broken reeds and brush kneel down around each indent, but aside from a scant few clawed footprints leading to and fro, there is no indication that any of these river-front residences are actively used -- or at least actively accessed from anywhere but the water.

node.name: The Faldesu River, Shallows
node.description: Reeds and cattails clog the shallow water, providing shelter for an assortment of small, wide-eyed fish that dart energetically just below the surface. A steady rustle of wind off the Faldesu sets the thick tops of the cattails into motion, drumming off one another in the air's invisible caress and revealing glimpses of woodland vegetation further to the west. From here, there is no access -- only knee-deep mud and nettles.

node.name: Outside Throne City, Faldesu Inlet
node.description: Wide-leafed plants fill the edges of the inlet like the spectators at a parade, waving their fronds as if in admiration of the gurgling waters. Flowing on its course, the water is indifferent to this constant wind-driven attention, content to be the source of life for so much vegetation and then to offer it nothing more. If adoring gazes are what the plants give, then stoic resistance is the only gift from a series of stone pillars, half submerged in the liquid.

node.name: Outside Throne City, Faldesu Inlet
node.description: Where once ancient feet trod upon interlocked cobblestones, now thickened grass and choking weeds grow, narrowing the remains of a major trade road to a mere footpath. Rocks and light rubble lie buried in the thorny vegetation, an indication of a long-ago structure stretching toward a distant wall far into the woodland. All this abandoned ruin is of no concern to nature, her attention spent in the years since to grow a particularly large tree that leans over the nearby waterway.

node.name: Thayergraben, Faldesu Inlet
node.description: A twist of thorns and spiny plants hinders passage north but fails to block it completely. Dragonflies dart in and out of the tangle, back and forth between it and the neighboring reeds choking the inlet. Not all obstacles here are natural, as a large granite block lies in the water as well, and almost completely buried in vegetation along the shore is a granite marker of some kind.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Shallows
node.description: Narrow rows of reeds and cattails rise in anything but oppressive numbers, and certainly not in quantities large enough to alter the course of the inlet. The water is merely shallow and relatively easy to traverse, though the silt and mud does its best to impede. Jutting up from these measly depths are several stone pillars, all in a row.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Shallows
node.description: Clouds of silt drift beneath the surface of the shallow water, spinning and twirling like the tumultuous motion of a submerged storm. The water is not intensely powerful against anything more weighty, though the steady pressure has forced the brown and green reeds to angle downstream. Foam nestles at the stems of these bent aquatic plants, occasionally pulling free to sail unfettered with the current.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Shallows
node.description: Thick trees from the shore hang languidly over the inlet waters, drooping their willowy limbs down as if drinking. Perhaps they seek vainly to reach the large squarish stone beneath the surface, slicked with strings of submerged algae. The water itself is clear but dark, shaded by the mass of Shakoepi Bridge overhead. Small fish dart along the bottom, dancing from reed to reed in search of tasty leeches or insects.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Churning Waters
node.description: Significantly stronger here than the water just a few steps westward, the forceful current pushes far too hard for any thick vegetation to take hold. This is not the case on the far eastern shore, as immense trees and wide swaths of reeds and cattails clog the inlet waters. To reach that land from here would require crossing the deepest water of the inlet while risking a quick death by drowning.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Churning Waters
node.description: Flowing with such rapid force that swimming directly northward is impossible, the inlet's waters are far easier to manage at an angle. Bits of floatsam are carried swiftly by, lost and swirling until deposited upon some muddy bank. Some of this debris falls from the branches of a large, overhanging tree directly overhead. South, a pair of immense stone blocks squeezes the rushing waters into an even more hectic speed.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Granite Block
node.description: This granite block provides a stable if somewhat slick perch in the middle of the inlet's flowing waters. Several paces wide, it doesn't even slightly tilt nor tremble with the passage of rushing water. The size and resilience indicates it may have been from a particularly massive structure. An equally large granite rock lies in the calmer water to the east, although it looks extremely slippery and wet.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Slick Granite Rock
node.description: Though the inlet waters are not particularly rough here, foamy white splashes soak the surface of this large rock every few seconds. Years of this action have worn the granite smooth, and slippery green slime maximizes its slickness, providing a possible but hazardous crossing to the eastern bank. Perhaps once this rock was part of a larger structure, but the vanquished features are long since gone.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Eastern Bank
node.description: Thick water oaks cluster a short distance from the shore, growing with abandon in this rich, moist soil. Despite the packed reeds, grasses, and undergrowth, several criss-crossing paths divide and conjoin, generally heading southward. Someone or something regularly beats the vegetation into submission while passing.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Eastern Shore
node.description: Oak and willow trees spread their greenery overhead in a heavy umbrella of tangled branches and drooping limbs. In this shade, the shoreline is covered with layers of hairy moss, thriving in the dank dirt and drinking in the occasional spray of mist. Though this moss may provide a soft bed for tired beasts, the sharp reeds lining the inlet are a hazard for thirsty snouts.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Eastern Shore
node.description: Amidst the rough-barked trees, clusters of thin, wiry vines dangle from the upper limbs and wrap around the trunks. Some tinged with red along the leaves, others merely rough and cordlike, the vines belie the increasingly feral nature of this shore. Further south, the woodland becomes so choked with hearty plant life that only subtle signs indicate the presence of humanity.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Eastern Shore
node.description: Patches of elephant grass and clumps of dagger-like reeds part just enough to reveal easy access to the inlet shallows, though what may lurk in those dark waters could be worse than anything hiding in the vegetation. Through this vacancy can be seen swifter waters to the north and in the middle of the inlet, but close to the shore it slogs along slowly, churning up mud and leeches. The nearest ground is waterlogged, blurring the line between land and liquid.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet, Shallows
node.description: Clusters of brown cattails clog the water, leaving scant few spaces available for wading explorers to trudge. The current beneath the surface is routed and rerouted, forced between the shoots of these water-dwelling reeds, but nonetheless black and thick with swirling mud. Strong, deadly, and deep water lies further to the west, beckoning but cordoned off by rows of hardy plants.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet
node.description: Slowing somewhat as it approaches the shore, the inlet takes on a distinctive, lazy-river feel, though the undercurrent remains a continual risk. The water is murky, churned mud along the bottom rising nearly to the surface. What hungry things might lurk in this cloudy flow can only be guessed, but there are plenty of hiding spaces for who knows what.

node.name: Faldesu Inlet
node.description: Though the water is quite deep and the current rather strong like a small pony, masses of aquatic vegetation manage to thrive. For the most part, the long tendrils of algae-coated grasses cling to the sheltering sides of two massive blocks in the course of the inlet, but a few manage to survive just fine along the sticky sludge on the bottom.

node.name: Outside Throne City, Boggy Pit
node.description: Grey mud slides down the sides of this muck-filled pit, dripping off of roots and fallen tree limbs in great clumps. Though the water isn't particularly clean or clear due to this, it is transparent enough to see animal bones beneath the surface. Small worm-like things dart beneath the surface, apparently stirred into activity by any motion of falling mud or beast.

node.name: Phelim's Sanctum, Walk of Pillars
node.description: Columns made of a dark blue, almost ebony marble line the wide hall, stretching from the floor to nearly vanish in the darkness far above. Constellations of gold inlay dot the ceiling, visible despite the shadow, lending the illusion of a star-filled summer sky. Near the end of the hall, a powerfully sculpted statue of Phelim stands, head cast upward as if admiring the tribute to the heavens.

node.name: Phelim's Sanctum, Tear of Grazhir
node.description: A sprawling mosaic of bright colors has been tiled into the floor and spirals outwards in a tremendous sunburst shape from the center of the room. Though this may be a design feature solely to further enhance the remarkable brilliance of the design, nothing else here displays such shades and hues, as the walls and ceiling are a drab blue. A tiny white shard of mineral lies in the center of the mosaic.

node.name: Jewel of the Faldesu, Gems and Fine Metals
node.description: What is most striking about this home-turned-business is the true elegance and refinement of the decor. From the delicate mother-of-pearl animals inlaid into the rich rosewood of the crown molding to the ornamental framed portrait behind the counter, this is a high class establishment. Appearing somewhat out of place, a number of surly guards constantly linger, each keeping a cautious eye always turned to the customers.

node.name: Gealeranendae College, Tear of Grazhir Exhibit
node.description: Rising and vanishing into darkness, the circular walls are home to four marble gargoyles that stare balefully from their perches at the silvery-white shard imbedded deeply into the floor's center. A single plaque of brass and silver is fastened in the stones near the exit. Otherwise unadorned, the room is thick with shadows, no sconces or torches to help shed illumination.

node.name: Salt Yard, Barge Dock
node.description: The end of the dock is reinforced with heavy timbers and rusting iron plates, no doubt to compensate for the extreme weight of the high piles of salt sacks. Strategically hidden between the stacks are crates and leather satchels, their shipping labels designating their contents as decidely non-salty in origin. None of the items appear to be black-market goods, so why such lengths were taken to disguise their transport is not clear.

node.name: Tower Ruins, Ancient Military Post
node.description: Frail fingers of crumbling stone rise toward the sky, marking the places where once fortified walls held against threats of war and attacks. Near the tops of these half-collapsed piles, square-shaped vacancies remain as the only indication that this place ever had a roof or higher floors.

node.name: Garden Rooftop, Medical Pavilion
node.description: Branching vines of ivy creep outward from their tenacious hold on an ornamental trellis jutting up into view from below and wind through the elegantly beveled banisters of a low railing that borders the edge of the rooftop. Small clay pots nestle against the tendrils of green ivy, harboring carefully tended herbs that perfume the air with a faintly medicinal scent. Watching over the garden are three odd little statues, their ivory, jade and amber bodies mingling with the surrounding colors.

node.name: Garden Rooftop, Medical Pavilion
node.description: Occupying the center of the garden is a clear crystal dome enclosing a swirling mass of greenish mist. As though observing from on high, a lone figure carved from snow-white marble stands on its translucent perch, the long flowing tresses cascading towards the ground in sculptured smooth curves from an upward-tilted head, lost in contemplation. Bordering the edge of the roof is a low railing supported by elegantly beveled banisters, framing the tops of the high pillars lining the street below.

node.name: Garden Rooftop, Medical Pavilion
node.description: Sheltering a bed of roses the colors of the moons is a low railing resting atop elegantly beveled banisters. Tiles of green marble alternate with patches of springy heather and gildleaf shrubs, creating a verdant frame that compliments the blue and green of the serpents rising from within a marble fountain carved in bas-relief. Gazing toward a cobalt blue telescope from its perch in the water is a wooden duck, its wings partially extended as though for balance.

node.name: Imperial Palace, Conference Chambers
node.description: Hanging from the center of the vaulted ceiling is a massive gold and crystal chandelier, its gaethzen orbs glowing with a muted light. Below it, a large marble table stretches down the length of the room, accompanied by a full compliment of velvet-cushioned chairs. Portraits line the walls, most showing signs of water damage or minor ruin but all set in elegant wood frames.

node.name: Old Throne City, Vagabond's Row
node.description: Rubbish and blankets are strewn across the alleyway, possibly left by littering hands or blown by the wind. A tarp-covered statue stands near the street. Between this marble monstrosity and the empty crates stacked nearby, it is unlikely that passers-by would notice this area at all. Serving as one side of this man-made canyon is the wall of the nearby museum, a makeshift wooden scaffolding ascending its surface.

node.name: Museum of Imperial History, Rooftop
node.description: By day, the view would certainly be a commanding one, but now consists only of the distant twinkling lights of passing boats on the Faldesu. Much closer, a makeshift wooden scaffolding peaks at the edge of the wall, very nearly matching the height of the museum itself.

node.name: Museum of Imperial History, Northern Rooftop
node.description: Though the ornamentation on the roof's edge effectively forms a parapet, the street below can be surveyed easily by quick glances between the stone alfars and soldiers. Anyone on Dahl Aeliy would be hard pressed to spot a lurker hiding on this building, and such a task would be even harder for those living in the luxurious residential areas further north. From the rooftops, leisurely study of Throne City's layout can be taken for granted.

node.name: Museum of Imperial History, Rooftop
node.description: Shady figures move along other rooftops, their faces hidden by deep cloaks of various sullen hues. Luckily for anyone at this vantage point, the forms take no notice of other rooftops as their attentions are aimed downward and south. They blink in and out of view, appearing on other buildings, moving in utter silence like ghosts, their goals equally ethereal.

node.name: Museum of Imperial History, Southern Rooftop
node.description: The mingling, contrary scents of roses and horses arise from below, drifting from private gardens nestled behind the Palace as well as the nearby palatial stalls. Though ivy and flowering vines cover the stone, the walls of the Palace are notably unrestored, bearing centuries of chips and marks. Those who did the restoration certainly never intended to impress anyone who might be on the rooftop of the museum.
